State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95814 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660        322 5660            322 5601            322 6441         322 6441


December 21, 1978


                                                          78-12-138

Dan Garvin, Director
Community Development Department
City of Tracy
P.O. Box 1039
Tracy, CA  95376

Dear Mr. Garvin:

     This letter is in response to your correspondence of
November 6, 1978 concerning a potential conflict of interest
by a former city employee. You should be advised that
Government Code Section 3627 was repealed by the Political
Reform Act and is no longer in effect.

     Currently, there is no similar provision in the Political
Reform Act. However, the Commission is considering the pos-
sibility of proposing legislation which would restrict for
some period of time the activities of former public employees.

If I may be of further assistance, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        [illegible]
                                        Ted Prim, Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:nrw

CITY OF TRACY

P. O. BOX 1579 · TRACY · CALIFORNIA 95376 Area Code 209: 835-221


November 6, 1978


Ted Prim, Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division
POB 807
Sacramento CA 95804

Dear Mr. Prim:

Thank you very much for your expeditious handling of my request for
an opinion regarding a public conflict of interest in your letter dated
November 2nd.

I apologize for not being more explicit in my request for an informal
opinion. Specifically, my request relates to prohibitions set forth in
Section 3627 of the Government Code (enclosed). Depending on your in-
terpretation of the definition of the term "public official," my reading
of subsections (n), (b), (c) and (d) is that former public officials
shall not receive compensation from anyone whose "economic interests
were the subject of or affected by any action or decision in which he
participated, or was involved in in his official capacity during the period
of his services or employment." These sections seem to indicate that
Albert/Noble and Dingson could not receive compensation from any private
development firm having a contract with the City of Tracy for a redevelop-
ment project.

Thank you again for your attention to this matter.

Yours truly,

[signature]
DAN GARVIN, Director
Community Development Department


bb
encl

November 2, 1978


Dan Garvin, Director
Tracy Redevelopment Agency
P.O. Box 1029
Tracy, CA 95376

Dear Mr. Garvin:

     Thank you for your letter of October 11, 1978, regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts as I understand them are as follows.

     Mr. Diangson previously was employed by the Redevelopment Agency for the City of Tracy. Due to Proposition 13 budget cuts, he was laid off. Subsequently, he was employed by Albert/Noble which is preparing to submit bids on several redevelopment projects. You asked whether Mr. Diangson's private employment with Albert/Noble constitutes a conflict of interest and whether the city may be exposed to liability if it accepts a bid by Albert/Noble.

     The Political Reform Act does not regulate the activities of retired public officials. Thus, Mr. Diangson is free to accept any job in the private sector without fear of violating the Political Reform Act. If Mr. Diangson were still a public employee, the Act would require disclosure and disqualification with respect to any private financial interests which he could affect in his official capacity. See Government Code Sections 87100 et seq. and 87300 et. seq.

     With respect to your second question, the City generally would not be exposed to liability under the Political Reform Act by virtue of acceptance of a bid from Albert/Noble on a redevelopment project. However, if a present city employee violated the Political Reform Act in the course of acting on the bid application, the employee risks criminal and civil

Mr. Dan Garvin
November 2, 1978                                                Page Two


prosecution. Moreover, if a city employee involved in the
bid process were found to have a conflict of interest, the
courts could negate the acceptance of the bid and require
persons from review any conflicts of interest to review the
bid applications. However, the mere fact that a former
city employee has helped to prepare a bid application does
not expose the city to liability under the Political Reform
Act.

If you have any further questions, please contact me.

                                                    Sincerely,


                                                    Ted Prim, Chief
                                                    Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:nc

TRACY Redevelopment Agency

October 11, 1978

Michael Bennett, Executive Director
State of California Fair Political
Practices Commission
POB 807
Sacramento CA 95804

Subject: City of Tracy Redevelopment Agency

Dear Mr. Bennett:

The City of Tracy's Redevelopment Agency is just now, after two years,
digging itself out from underneath litigation (Citizen's Rights Committee:
Chris Piloras, et al vs. the City of Tracy) and Proposition 13. We hope to be
free and clear of all encumbrances and to be able to proceed with our rede-
velopment project by the first of 1979. The city does not, therefore, wish
to be bogged down in court again.

The reason for this letter is to seek your informal opinion, in addition
to the opinion of the City Attorney, concerning what we believe to be a con-
flict of interest that may be developing. The following outlines the reasons
for our concern.

As a result of Proposition 13 impact, Walter Diangson, Principal Planner,
Special Project Coordinator and Deputy Director of the Redevelopment Agency,
was laid off. Mr. Diangson had had, prior to his departure, the primary task
of writing the redevelopment plans and working with various consultants to
implement those plans. Among those consultants, was the firm of Albert/Noble.

Recently, the city has been contacted by Mr. Diangson and Albert/Noble, act-
ing together, with a proposal to be the prime developer for the redevelopment
project. The City Attorney, City Manager and I all believe that we, as indi-
viduals as well as the Redevelopment Agency, may be in a tenuous legal position
if we have any formal dealings with Mr. Diangson and Albert/Noble.

Our question is twofold: 1) does the action of Walter Diangson/Albert/
Noble constitute a conflict of interest; and, 2) what legal liability may accrue
to the City of Tracy Redevelopment Agency and to responsible staff persons if
we do business with the aforementioned group?

Attached are documents pertaining to the involvement of Mr. Diangson and
Albert/Noble. Included is the full redevelopment documentation, management
group ordinances and policies, a copy of Mr. Diangson's deposition in the
redevelopment case, a contract for services with Albert/Noble, and the most
recent billing for services by Albert/Noble.

P. O. BOX 1029 • TRACY, CALIFORNIA 95376 • PHONE (209) 835-2211

M. Bennett                                                    (2)
10/11/78

Thank you for your attention to this matter.  Please direct any questions to
me.

The deposition given by Mr. Diangson that is enclosed is one of two copies
available.  Because of its length, we would appreciate its return.

Yours truly,

[signature]
DAN GARVIN, Director
Community Development Director
